mission.title: Install and Configure Perses on Kubernetes
mission.description: Perses is a CNCF sandbox project that provides a dashboard tool for visualizing observability data from various sources like Prometheus, Tempo, Loki, and Pyroscope. Installing Perses allows you to create and manage dashboards that integrate multiple observability pillars in one place.

steps.title: Add the Perses Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Perses Helm repository to your Helm client to access the charts.
```bash
helm repo add perses https://charts.perses.dev
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Perses using Helm
steps.description:
Install Perses in a new namespace called 'perses' with the specified version.
```bash
helm install perses perses/perses --namespace perses --create-namespace --version v0.53.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Perses pods are running successfully in the 'perses' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace perses
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access the Perses dashboard
steps.description:
Set up port-forwarding to access the Perses dashboard locally.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/perses --namespace perses 8080:80
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
You may want to configure resource limits for the Perses deployment. Edit the values.yaml file or use Helm to set resource limits.
```bash
helm upgrade perses perses/perses --namespace perses --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Perses will show the pods running in the 'perses' namespace, and you will be able to access the dashboard at http://localhost:8080. You can also confirm that resource limits are applied by checking the deployment configuration.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add perses https://charts.perses.dev
helm repo update
helm install perses perses/perses --namespace perses --create-namespace --version v0.53.0
helm upgrade perses perses/perses --namespace perses --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
